title: Countly
description: Open-source, privacy-focused product analytics platform for tracking web, mobile and desktop apps with funnels, retention, crashes and push.
installation: Countly Community Edition is a multi-process stack. This template runs the PRIMARY all-in-one image countly/countly-server, which bundles Nginx plus BOTH Node.js processes (the API ingestion process and the frontend dashboard) behind a single HTTP port 80. DATASTORE: Countly stores everything in MongoDB. There is no native MongoDB add-on, so this template provisions the MongoDB-wire-compatible 'documentdb' add-on (Microsoft DocumentDB, SCRAM-SHA-256 + TLS, user mongoadmin). COMPATIBILITY CAVEAT: Countly targets genuine MongoDB and uses some Mongo-specific server behaviour; DocumentDB covers the common wire protocol but full feature parity is not guaranteed - if you hit issues, point COUNTLY_CONFIG__MONGODB at an external real MongoDB 6.x/7.x instance instead and drop the add-on. The connection is supplied as a full URI via COUNTLY_CONFIG__MONGODB (note the DOUBLE underscores - this is Countly's universal config-override prefix; per-process equivalents are COUNTLY_CONFIG_API__ / COUNTLY_CONFIG_FRONTEND__). ROTATE before production: the documentdb add-on password (default 'countly') and keep the URI in sync. FIRST-RUN: open the site, complete the on-screen setup wizard to create the global admin account, then add your first application to get its App Key/API Key for SDK integration. COMPANION / SCALE-OUT SERVICES (NOT deployed by this single-container template): for horizontal scale Countly splits into separate images - countly/api (API ingestion, listens on port 3001) and countly/frontend (dashboard, listens on port 6001) - each pointed at the same MongoDB via COUNTLY_CONFIG__MONGODB; scale countly/api replicas to match write traffic. The all-in-one image also runs internal cron/aggregation and plugin jobs in-process, so no separate scheduler/worker container is required for a single-node install. The mounted /opt/countly/frontend/express/public/appimages volume keeps uploaded app icons/avatars; plugin and config state otherwise lives in MongoDB.
